chapters.title: Are we doomed to die, or is there a life jacket?
chapters.text:
The future of humankind becomes bleaker every second. Humans are thoughtlessly ruining the planet by cutting down forests, polluting the air and oceans, over-consuming, killing and selling wild animals and plants, causing diseases and loss of biodiversity, and producing waste that will not decompose for centuries to come. The main question is: will those centuries even come? At the rate we are destroying our home, the finite sources of our planet are bound to run out and leave us to die. We forget that our lives rely on nature, and it is nature that controls us, not the other way around.

The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, but in ourselves, that we are underlings. ~ William Shakespeare

We live in very difficult times when everything is going off at once — environmental crisis, wars, threats to human rights and democracy, violence, racism, inequality. Sometimes it seems that people fighting these issues are just wasting time. Jane Goodall disagrees; having lived through World War II, the Cold War, the 9/11 disaster, school shootings, and suicide bombings, she never gave up on the world, no matter how much despair and cruelty she witnessed. You might think she just has a strong personality that brought her through, and while that also may be true, the main reason behind her relentless optimism is hope.

She believes that despite the enormity of harm humans have caused to the planet, there is still a chance to avoid disastrous consequences. But the actions must be immediate: people worldwide should stock up on hope and cooperate in restoring the balance between humanity and nature. Each person can make a difference. No matter our social status, country, age, line of work, or any other factors, we all influence the world by our everyday decisions as mundane as which milk to buy. Imagine the difference it would make if every person on the planet planted a tree: the world would instantly have more forests. That way, millions of eco-friendly decisions have an enormously positive effect on the planet.

Regardless of how bad things are, even the worst-case scenario can be changed for the better.

chapters.title: Finding out more about hope
chapters.text:
People give up and become passive and ignorant without hope. And when we ignore our problems instead of fixing them, they pile up and eventually overwhelm us. Hope is not the kind of wishful thinking that some people believe it to be, like “hoping” that the apples will grow on a tree without even planting a seed. Hope is about acting while believing in the best outcome: having a vivid image in your mind and doing something to make it real.

Unlike wishful thinking, hope acknowledges the obstacles that are in the way and, at the same time, focuses on the desired outcome. We owe hope for our survival, as, without hope, humans would come to terms with any seemingly inevitable disaster and would not be motivated to try and change it.

Research points out that certain genetic predispositions can result in an optimistic personality. However, the early development and environment play a big part in the overall view of life: people born in war zones and poor or abusive households would surely have a more pessimistic outlook despite their genetic inheritance. And vice versa: people without said dispositions can develop a more optimistic perception due to a high quality of life.

Hope can be cultivated and is not a fixed variable, such as DNA.

These are the four required components to maintain hope:
• Having realistic goals
• Finding possible ways to take action
• Being confident in one's success
• Receiving support from others

When we realize that we can make a difference, we become determined to act. We allow ourselves to act by envisioning the ways we can meet our goals. Drafting out specifically what we want to achieve should eliminate the anxiety from trying to deal with more than we can handle. And we become much more empowered when we feel supported by our friends and like-minded individuals. That is the secret recipe for maintaining hope in life.

When the future becomes too grim, remember that no person in the world can predict what's to come, and anything can happen.

Jane Goodall's hopefulness revolves around four main factors: human intellect, the power of youth, the resilience of nature, and the unyielding human spirit. The next chapters will provide in-depth explanations of why these specific reasons give her hope.

chapters.title: Reason one: human intellect
chapters.text:
Dr. Louis Leakey was the paleontologist who took an interest in chimpanzees and let Jane Goodall undertake an expedition to observe their behavior, which was a dream come true for her since she loved nature and wild animals. Understanding the behavioral patterns of chimpanzees — creatures that humans share 99% of their DNA with — would be beneficial to know humans better.

The first months were not helpful as the chimpanzees would run away as soon as they saw her. She found a high spot to observe them from a distance then. It seemed useless, but Jane persevered. That is when she made her greatest discovery: a chimp she was observing used a stick to fish out termites from a mound. The sole fact that the chimpanzees could use improvised materials as tools to achieve their goals completely undermined the misconception that humans were the only creatures on Earth capable of that.

For a long time, humanity considered animals to have barely any intellect. However, modern science has already discovered that this was a mistake. Goodall and other scientists have proven that human and animal intellects are in constant development. Chimpanzees can remember words and solve problems, pigs like to draw, octopuses have a brain in each of their eight arms, and rats are very smart.

Our intellect is merely a tool. It's how we choose to use it that matters.

However, there is a great difference between intelligence and intellect. The closest human relatives in the animal kingdom, such as chimpanzees, are indeed intelligent. But they cannot create rockets and invent robots as humans do due to their developed intellect. However, an intelligent ape would never destroy his home, while people knowingly continue to harm the only place that they inhabit — the Earth.

Goodall believes that humans are exceptional in that we are capable of both true evil — carefully planning horrible things knowing that they are immoral, and altruism — helping other people while taking risks for oneself. However, humans will never be able to unlock their full potential unless they combine the thinking of the brain and the feelings of the heart.

chapters.title: Reason two: nature’s resilience
chapters.text:
Besides being an expert on primates, Jane Goodall is knowledgeable about plants. When you think about it, animals and humans rely on flora for their survival: food, shelter, clarity of air, and water. We need nature, but it does not need us. It regenerates and thrives without our intervention, and it is resilient; some trees have managed to survive for 200 million years! Life is resilient, but since humans have already inflicted so much harm to nature, it is our responsibility to use our intellect to help it out in restoring itself.

We cannot live on if the natural world dies. ~ Jane Goodall

Thankfully, rewilding initiatives are now sprouting up all over the world, working towards restoring oceans, forests, and other ecosystems once destroyed by humans. Some animals are even saved from the brink of extinction. For instance, wildlife biologist Dr. Don Merton saved the male and female black robins. The black-footed ferret, the Iberian lynx, whooping crane, milu deer, and Arabian oryx are only a few examples of species saved thanks to successful breeding programs.

Humanity has already exterminated 60% of all reptiles, mammals, fish, and birds that ever existed.

You cannot help animals unless you help humans first. All the species on this planet are interconnected and depend on each other. This is why any loss of biodiversity negatively influences everyone on the planet. For example, when chimpanzees were going extinct in some countries of Africa, Jane Goodall traveled there to find out why. It turned out that people living in those areas were so poor that they did not have any other choice than to exploit nature for charcoal, wood, and other resources, thereby making the area uninhabitable for the chimpanzees. However, the effects would not be lasting if Goodall were to help out chimpanzees alone without providing help to humans. That led her organization, Tacare, to operate in seven African countries educating villagers about agroforestry and implementing tree-planting initiatives to maintain ecological balance.

Did you know? Trees have an underground web of roots that allows them to exchange information about their health or needs.

chapters.title: Reason three: the power of youth
chapters.text:
If we consider what state the Earth is in now due to previous generations' harmful exploitation of the planet, it should be no surprise that many young people now are devastated and depressed. We have indeed compromised our children's future! But according to Jane Goodall, humanity still has hope. She has discovered that with a little bit of support and a few heartfelt stories about the wrong things in the world, children can also take action, and their energy and persistence are unmatched. The Fridays for Future organization created by Greta Thunberg and the Roots & Shoots youth-led global community are brilliant examples of young people's determination to make a difference and fix things.

Gardening with other people is a great way to connect, learn about nature, and contribute to ecology.

Today's youth is much more aware of all the environmental issues than previous generations. Growing up, these children could not avoid hearing about deforestation, pollution, and global warming from the news, television, and radio. And as adults, they become more engaged in politics and decision-making, vote for presidents that, in their opinion, would take measures to protect the ecology, and start to change the world for the better in any way they can.

But children are not the only ones who should carry the burden of the fight against the ecological crisis. It should be a universal, collective movement with adults doing what they can from their positions of power. Only by combining the efforts of all, old and young, can we avert the destruction of our planet. One thing that adults can do now is to support young people in their aspirations and activism. Human support is one of the components of maintaining hope, after all. As Jane Goodall noticed with chimpanzees, those chimps who had supportive parents became more successful later in life — an observation that easily applies to humans. We are leaving so many global issues for future generations to handle that the least we can do is cultivate their hope and effectively empower them to deal with the challenges.

chapters.title: Reason four: the unyielding human spirit
chapters.text:
People can exhibit hopefulness and determination even at the worst of times. So many stories of people surviving traumatic, terrible events in their life and moving on to achieving their goals prove nothing is impossible if you believe in yourself and cultivate your spirit. What doesn't kill us indeed makes us stronger, but for that to happen, you should allow your struggles to teach you instead of draining you in the ocean of self-pity and desperation.

Danger tends to awaken selflessness and altruism in people. Think about pacifists driving ambulances to the centers of war zones, suffragettes throwing themselves in front of a royal horse just to point out the extent of female oppression, German civilians hiding Jewish children during the Nazi invasion, or ordinary people saving wild animals from forest fires in Australia and California. Such resilience indeed happens, but not to all people: some fall into the pit of depression and desperation. What plays a key role here is whether you are an optimist or a pessimist.

Helping others allows you to heal faster.

Social support is of paramount importance when it comes to being resilient and overcoming trauma. We are social beings, after all. A few kind words from a friend can do wonders in a tough situation. Just knowing someone is looking after you can boost your confidence to no end.

Even though we realize the drastic consequences of our actions, the immensity of harm we have already caused to our environment pushes most people into hopelessness. Why bother if everything has already gone that far? There is even a new term, “eco-grief,” that describes feelings of sadness, depression, and fatality when thinking about the ecological crisis of our planet.

But it is better to be not ideal in caring about the planet than to do nothing at all. Any decision we take impacts Earth in a way, and there are so many of us. Even each person's small sacrifices gathered together will make a significant difference compared to a group of people who were not trying at all.

To cope with despair and the enormity of the problem, we should focus on the battles that we won and on the positive events happening around the globe thanks to the efforts of eco-activists and like-minded people. There are plenty of good stories out there to enlighten us and give us hope.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
We keep destroying our planet because we do not think compassionately of future generations that will have to face even more drastic environmental issues. We favor immediate benefits over the long-term well-being of the Earth.

We need to cultivate our hope, as the future is as good as lost without it. Thankfully, we have reasons to hope for a better outcome: we have our impeccable intellect capable of resolving issues and finding new ways to achieve our goals. Our youth is strong and full of energy, and they inspire others with their determination and involvement. Nature is wise and resilient and is capable of healing itself. Finally, our fighting spirit is unmatched and can withstand severe pressure.

The bitter news is that the current pandemic was predicted long ago by experts in zoonotic diseases. It was inevitable, considering how people continued abusing nature and animals for personal gain. However, we had an opportunity to see our world in a different light. When the pandemic happened, and lots of industries shut down, there was an instant decrease in carbon emissions, the air became clearer, noise reduced to the point where birds' chirping could be heard even in big cities, and the stars became more visible at night. We should not forget the valuable lessons we have learned as the future of humankind depends on them.

Try this

Go to the Fridays for Future website or any other eco-activist organization's website and subscribe to their newsletters, make a donation, and read the articles they post. See when they have the next strike for saving the climate and join them. Such actions do not require too much effort, yet they still are important and contribute to the global fight for the future of the Earth.
